content.full_text:
- 17

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660              322-5901            322-6441              322-6444

April 16, 1981

Betty L. Wallis
Deputy City Clerk
401 East Chapman Avenue
Placentia, CA 92670

Dear Ms. Wallis:

     Thank you for your letter requesting a confirmation of
telephone advice given by a member of our staff.

     Based on your letter and our phone conversation, my
understanding of the facts is as follows. Two council members
are presently the subject of a recall effort. The recall
petitions are due to be submitted by May 7, 1981; the earliest
the petitions could be certified is June, and the election
would be held between 89 and 100 days after the date of
certification, or sometime in September at the earliest.
The two council members have sued the recall committee,
named individual members of the committee, and 100 John Does
for libel and slander. It is alleged in the lawsuit that
the contents of the recall petition and statements made by
the committee and individual committee members during the
course of the recall campaign are libelous and slanderous.
The council members are expending personal funds to finance
the lawsuit; no committees or legal funds have been set up
to solicit contributions or make expenditures to further the
lawsuit.

     The question you raised is whether the council members'
expenditures of personal funds in this lawsuit constitute
reportable campaign expenditures within the meaning of the
Political Reform Act and Commission regulations.

     My conclusions, which confirm the telephone advice, is
that these are reportable campaign expenditures based on the
following analysis.

82025

Betty L. Wallis
April 16, 1981
Page Two


     Government Code Section 82025^1/ defines an expenditure
as "a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan
by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment,
unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that
it is not made for political purposes."^2/ Expenditure in-
cludes "any monetary or nonmonetary payment made by a
candidate, unless it is clear from surrounding circumstances
that the payment was made for personal purposes unrelated to
his candidacy or status as an officeholder..." 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18225(b).

     In 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(a), it is explained
that "'[p]olitical purposes,' as used in Government Code
Section 82025, means made for the purpose of influencing or
attempting to influence the action of the voters for or
against the nomination or election of a candidate or candi-
dates, or the qualification or passage of any measure."

     Accordingly, based on the Act and the Regulations, the
test for the reportability of the use of an officeholder's
personal funds for legal expenses is whether or not, under
all the circumstances, the litigation is being carried out
for purposes related to the election process.^3  See opinion
requested by Douglas Buchanan, 4 FPPC 14 (No. 78-013, May 1,
1979). As the Commission noted in Buchanan, it is impossible
to formulate a rule for the reporting of legal expenditures
since it will ultimately depend on the particular facts of
each case.

     In this case, we find that the expenditures are related
to the election process based on all of the circumstances
including the following:

     1.  The lawsuit arises out of the language of the
recall petition and statements made by the recall committee;

     2.  The lawsuit seems to be part of the council
members' attempt to retain their public office; and


_____________________________

     ^1/  All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise stated.

     ^2/  Sections 84200, et seq. govern the reporting of
expenditures by candidates, committees and elected officers.

     ^3/  The definition of election includes any recall
election.  Government Code Section 82022.

Betty L. Wallis
April 16, 1981
Page Three


     3.  The lawsuit is part of the councilmembers'
opposition to an attempt to qualify a ballot measure
since the allegations of the lawsuit are that the state-
ments in the recall petition and by the recall committee
are false.

     Therefore, the expenditures made by the council members
to further this lawsuit are reportable campaign expenditures.

     As you know, the activities of the recall committee are
also subject to the reporting requirements of the Act.

     Please feel free to contact me if you would like to
discuss this letter or if you have further questions.

                                   Very truly yours,

                                   [signature]

                                   Diane Maura-Fishburn
                                   Counsel
                                   Legal Division

DMF:kp

COUNTY ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICE

COUNTY OF HUMBOLDT
EUREKA, CALIFORNIA 95501 PHONE (707) 445-7266

March 23, 1981


Ms. Stella Levy
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street Mall
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Ms. Levy:

Recently, one of your staff called to arrange a meeting with
me pursuant to an investigation underway regarding one of the
Eureka City Councilmen. I learned that appointments had been
scheduled also with County Counsel, Board Members and apparently
other staff as well. These appointments and, I assume the trip,
were subsequently cancelled although it was widely reported here
by the local news media.

A number of inquiries have been made of the Commission from the
Humboldt County area. This along with the extensive media
coverage of your cancelled trip up here, have left many of our
local elected officials with the feeling that any or all of
them may be under some unnamed suspicion and under investigation
by unknown agencies.

I ask your assistance in preparing a response that will clear
up the issue for the Board of Supervisors. Responses to the
following questions will certainly aid me in putting the issue
to rest.

1. What inquiries or complaints have you received from
   this area and who originated the inquiries or complaints?

2. What organizations or individuals are currently under
   investigation in Humboldt County?

3. What are your procedures for responding to inquiries and
   for conducting investigations?

4. What findings, if any, have you made to date?

Frank answers to the above questions would be of great value
in clearing up the innuendoes and rumors currently playing
well in this community. I await your response.

Sincerely,

ROBERT E. HENDRIX
County Administrative Officer

REH/bd

COUNTY ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICE

COUNTY OF HUMBOLDT
EUREKA, CALIFORNIA 95501    PHONE (707) 445-7266

March 23, 1981


Ms. Stella Levy
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street Mall
Sacramento, California  95814

Dear Ms. Levy:

Recently, one of your staff called to arrange a meeting with
me pursuant to an investigation underway regarding one of the
Eureka City Councilmen.  I learned that appointments had been
scheduled also with County Counsel, Board Members and apparently
other staff as well.  These appointments and, I assume the trip,
were subsequently cancelled although it was widely reported here
by the local news media.

A number of inquiries have been made of the Commission from the
Humboldt County area.  This along with the extensive media
coverage of your cancelled trip up here, have left many of our
local elected officials with the feeling that any or all of
them may be under some unnamed suspicion and under investigation
by unknown agencies.

I ask your assistance in preparing a response that will clear
up the issue for the Board of Supervisors.  Responses to the
following questions will certainly aid me in putting the issue
to rest.

1.  What inquiries or complaints have you received from
    this area and who originated the inquiries or complaints?

2.  What organizations or individuals are currently under
    investigation in Humboldt County?

3.  What are your procedures for responding to inquiries and
    for conducting investigations?

4.  What findings, if any, have you made to date?

Frank answers to the above questions would be of great value
in clearing up the innuendoes and rumors currently playing
well in this community.  I await your response.

Sincerely,

[signature]
ROBERT E. HENDRIX
County Administrative Officer

REH/bd

Mayor
NORMAN Z. ECKENRODE

City Administrator
EDWIN T. POWELL

The People Are The City

PLACENTIA
ALL AMERICA CITY

Councilmembers
DONALD A. HOLT, JR.
BETTY MEAD
RICHARD ACTON
PETER LABORDE

401 East Chapman Avenue · Placentia, California 92670

February 27, 1981

Mr. Rich Eichman
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Eichman:

As we discussed on February 25, 1981, this office is requesting
a written confirmation of the determination by your offices of
the following question which has been posed by one of our
Councilmembers in regard to the present recall attempt against
her.

Does the following expenditure constitute a
reportable campaign expenditure under the regulations
of the Political Reform Act?

The filing of a lawsuit (by a Councilmember being
sought for recall) against the recall committee,
named individuals and 200 unnamed "John Does" on
the basis that the recall efforts are libelous and
slanderous.

During our conversation of February 25, 1981, you cited
2 Cal Admin Code Section 18225 and Government Code Section
82025 as supporting the requirement that the above situation
constitutes a reportable campaign expenditure. The Councilmember involved in the situation is now requesting a written
confirmation of this finding.

Sincerely,

DORIS B. BLACK
CITY CLERK

By: Betty L. Wallis
Deputy City Clerk

cc: Councilmember Mead

George P. Bran
Attorney at Law
112 No. Bradford
Placentia, California 92670                                (714) 524-0380

May 5, 1981


Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mrs. Fisburn:

I am in receipt of a copy of your letter of April 16, 1981,
addressed to Betty L. Wallis, Deputy City Clerk, in which you
conclude that the lawsuit by my clients, two council members,
who are presently the subject of a recall effort, are political
expenditures requiring reporting. I do not agree with your
analysis for the following reasons:

    1. The lawsuit arises out of defamatory statements
published by certain persons, who ultimately became members of
a recall committee, and whose statements became incorporated
into the Petition for Recall. The committee subsequently caused
the defamatory statements to be published and re-published.

    2. The lawsuit is not a part of council members
attempt to retain their public office, but is a suit for
recovery of damages for injury to their reputation.     Nor is it
part of the council members opposition to an attempt to qualify
a ballot measure.

    3. Pursuant to the Administrative Code Section
18225 (a) Political Purposes as used in the Government Code,
section 82025 means made for the purpose of influencing or
attempting to influence the actions of voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates..."In the
instant suit, the suit is not directed against influencing the
action of voters as to whether they should vote for or against
the nomination or election of any candidate or candidates.     It
is directed directly against persons who have published
defamatory statements about my clients. The public is free to
sign petitions for recall without threat of any nature.

In no way is the suit related to the nomination for election of a
candidate nor toward qualification or passage of any measure.

page 2

The litigation is being carried out for purposes not related to
the election process. The fact that the statements are contained
in the recall petition is incidental to the litigation. The
litigation would have been carried out for the same defamatory
statements had they not ultimately been contained in a recall
petitions.

The entire action is a private suit against private individuals
concerning violation of private rights. There are no public
rights involved.

I believe the opinion which you have rendered goes beyond the
definition of political purposes as defined in the California
Administrative Code and is not applicable to this particular
lawsuit.

It would seem to me that it would have been appropriate to
contact this office to determine exactly what the nature of
the suit was before rendering your opinion on such.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
George R. Bean

GRB:klf

CC: Doris B. Blark, City Clerk
    Council Member Mead
    Council Member Laborde

sections.question_synthetic: Whether expenditures of personal funds by two council members being recalled to finance a defamation lawsuit against the recall committee and its members constitute reportable campaign expenditures under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that the legal expenditures are reportable campaign expenditures because the lawsuit arises from the recall petition language and statements, is part of the council members' attempt to retain office, and opposes qualification of a ballot measure (the recall election).
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes primary advice letter (April 16, 1981) with clear QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections. Also includes supporting correspondence: initial request from City Clerk (February 27, 1981), unrelated letter from Humboldt County (March 23, 1981), and attorney response disputing the FPPC determination (May 5, 1981). Main advice letter is well-structured with statutory analysis.

Based on your letter and our phone conversation, my understanding of the facts is as follows. Two council members are presently the subject of a recall effort. The recall petitions are due to be submitted by May 7, 1981; the earliest the petitions could be certified is June, and the election would be held between 89 and 100 days after the date of certification, or sometime in September at the earliest. The two council members have sued the recall committee, named individual members of the committee, and 100 John Does for libel and slander. It is alleged in the lawsuit that the contents of the recall petition and statements made by the committee and individual committee members during the course of the recall campaign are libelous and slanderous. The council members are expending personal funds to finance the lawsuit; no committees or legal funds have been set up to solicit contributions or make expenditures to further the lawsuit. The question you raised is whether the council members' expenditures of personal funds in this lawsuit constitute reportable campaign expenditures within the meaning of the Political Reform Act and Commission regulations. My conclusions, which confirm the telephone advice, is that these are reportable campaign expenditures based on the following analysis. 82025 Betty L. Adm. Code Section 18225(b). In 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(a), it is explained that "'[p]olitical purposes,' as used in Government Code Section 82025, means made for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or candi- dates, or the qualification or passage of any measure." embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel Diane Maura Fishburn advises Deputy City Clerk Betty L. Wallis that personal funds expended by council members for a defamation lawsuit against a recall committee constitute reportable campaign expenditures under the Political Reform Act. The determination is based on the lawsuit's direct relation to the election process and the council members' candidacy status.
